preamble: The complete loss or elision of a voiceless aspirated alveolar affricate, resulting in a zero sound, represents an extreme form of lenition. This phonetic transformation can occur in specific historical periods or phonetic environments, often as part of broader sound changes within a language's phonological evolution.

examples.note: The Sumerian phoneme /ř/, reconstructed by some scholars as a voiceless aspirated alveolar affricate [tsʰ], underwent a gradual loss of its independent phonemic status, eventually reducing to zero in standard cuneiform writing.

sources: Jagersma, B. (2006). A Descriptive Grammar of Sumerian. PhD dissertation, Leiden University.

Sallaberger, W. (2024). Lugal-šà-lá-tuku: Glimpses into the Career of an Old Sumerian Chief… In From the 25th Century BC to the 25th Century AD: Studies in Honour of Aage Westenholz. Edizioni Ca’ Foscari.
